# Path-to-RegExp

[![NPM version][npm-image]][npm-url]
[![Build status][travis-image]][travis-url]
[![Test coverage][coveralls-image]][coveralls-url]
[![Dependency Status][david-image]][david-url]
[![License][license-image]][license-url]

> Turn an Express-style path string such as `/user/:name` into a regular expression.

## Installation

```
npm install path-to-regexp --save
```

## Usage

```javascript
var pathToRegexp = require('path-to-regexp');

// Using strings:
var keys = [];
var re = pathToRegexp('/foo/:bar', keys);
// re = /^\/foo\/([^\/]+?)\/?$/i
// keys = [{ name: 'bar', prefix: '/', delimiter: '/', optional: false, repeat: false, pattern: '[^\/]+?' }]

// Using array of paths:
var re = pathToRegexp(['/foo/:bar', '/foo/:baz']);

// Using RegExp:
var re = pathToRegexp(/^\/(foo|bar)$/i);

// Using an array of RegExps and strings:
var re = pathToRegexp([/^\/foo$/i, '\/bar']);
```

### Parameters

```javascript
var re = pathToRegexp(path, keys, options);
```

- **path** A string, array of strings, or a regular expression.
- **keys** An array to be populated with the keys found in the path.
- **options**
  - **sensitive** When `true` the regexp will be case sensitive. (default: `false`)
  - **strict** When `true` the regexp won't allow an optional trailing delimiter to match. (default: `false`)
  - **end** When `true` the regexp will match to the end of the string. (default: `true`)
  - **delimiter** The default delimiter for segments. (default: `/`)

## API

### pathToRegexp(path, [keys, options])

Return a regular expression based on the path provided.

```javascript
var keys = [];
var re = pathToRegexp('/foo/:bar', keys);
// re = /^\/foo\/([^\/]+?)\/?$/i
// keys = [{ name: 'bar', prefix: '/', delimiter: '/', optional: false, repeat: false, pattern: '[^\/]+?' }]
```

### tokensToRegExp(tokens, [options])

Take the tokens returned from `parse` and convert to a regular expression.

```javascript
var tokens = pathToRegexp.parse('/route/:foo/(.*)');
// tokens = [
//   '/route',
//   { name: 'foo', prefix: '/', delimiter: '/', optional: false, repeat: false, pattern: '[^\/]+?' },
//   '/',
//   { name: 0, prefix: '', delimiter: '', optional: false, repeat: false, pattern: '.*' }
// ]

var re = pathToRegexp.tokensToRegExp(tokens);
// re = /\/route\/([^\/]+?)\/(.*)\/?$/i
```

### parse(path)

Parse a path into an array of tokens.

```javascript
var tokens = pathToRegexp.parse('/route/:foo/(.*)');
// tokens = [
//   '/route',
//   { name: 'foo', prefix: '/', delimiter: '/', optional: false, repeat: false, pattern: '[^\/]+?' },
//   '/',
//   { name: 0, prefix: '', delimiter: '', optional: false, repeat: false, pattern: '.*' }
// ]
```

## Express-Style Path Specification

This module is used by Express for route matching and route generation. Path strings used in Express have the following syntax:

### Parameters

Named parameters are defined by prefixing a colon to the parameter name (`:foo`). By default, the parameter will match until the next delimiter is found.

```
/user/:name
```

Matches:

```
/user/tj
/user/hello
```

Does not match:

```
/user/
/user/tj/hello
```

#### Optional Parameters

Parameters can be suffixed with a question mark (`?`) to make the parameter optional.

```
/user/:name?
```

Matches:

```
/user
/user/tj
```

#### Zero-or-more Parameters

Parameters can be suffixed with an asterisk (`*`) to denote a zero-or-more parameter match.

```
/user/:name*
```

Matches:

```
/user
/user/tj
/user/tj/hello
```

#### One-or-more Parameters

Parameters can be suffixed with a plus sign (`+`) to denote a one-or-more parameter match.

```
/user/:name+
```

Matches:

```
/user/tj
/user/tj/hello
```

Does not match:

```
/user
```

### Custom Match Parameters

All parameters can be provided a custom matching regexp and override the default. Please note: Backslashes need to be escaped in strings.

```
/user/:id(\d+)
```

Matches:

```
/user/123
/user/456
```

Does not match:

```
/user/abc
/user/456/789
```

### Unnamed Parameters

It is possible to write an unnamed parameter that only consists of a regexp. It works the same as a named parameter, except it will be numerically indexed.

```
/user/(\d+)
```

Matches:

```
/user/123
/user/456
```

Does not match:

```
/user/abc
/user/456/789
```

## Examples in Express.js

Here are some examples of using path-to-regexp with Express.js for route matching:

```javascript
var express = require('express');
var app = express();

// Simple route with no parameters
app.get('/', function(req, res) {
  res.send('Hello, World!');
});

// Route with a named parameter
app.get('/user/:id', function(req, res) {
  res.send('User ID: ' + req.params.id);
});

// Route with an optional parameter
app.get('/product/:category?', function(req, res) {
  if (req.params.category) {
    res.send('Category: ' + req.params.category);
  } else {
    res.send('All products');
  }
});

// Route with a parameter with a custom regexp
app.get('/order/:id(\d+)', function(req, res) {
  res.send('Order ID: ' + req.params.id);
});

// Route with a wildcard parameter
app.get('/files/*', function(req, res) {
  res.send('File path: ' + req.params[0]);
});

app.listen(3000);
```
